title: Urbanization exacerbated the rainfall and flooding caused by hurricane Harvey in Houston
methods: Building Energy Model (BEM) urban canopy parameterization

data_scope.wrf_domains: 3 nested domains at 12 km, 4 km, and 1.33 km resolution

core_conclusion: Urbanization amplified both the total rainfall and the flood discharge of hurricane Harvey in Houston. WRF ensemble simulations comparing urban-present vs. urban-removed experiments, combined with nonstationary flood-frequency analysis across five watersheds, show that urbanization increased the probability of extreme flood events by approximately 21 times during 25-30 August 2017.
why_it_matters: Isolates urbanization from climate change as a distinct and quantifiable flood-amplification driver; demonstrates that cities modify not only runoff but precipitation itself; the 21x probability multiplier provides a concrete, policy-actionable risk estimate; findings generalize to all rapidly urbanizing coastal cities exposed to tropical cyclones.
why_nature: High-profile post-disaster attribution study for the costliest US natural disaster at the time; novel dual-evidence design (numerical simulation + nonstationary statistics); precise quantified risk estimate (21x) that is reproducible and falsifiable; calls for systemic change to how urban land use is represented in global climate models.
added_value_to_my_research: WRF ensemble pairs as training labels for a physics-informed GNN surrogate emulator predicting urbanization-flood amplification globally
Nonstationary GEV model with socioeconomic covariates -> spatial equity extension linking flood-risk amplification to urban disinvestment patterns
Dynamic urban morphology index from remote sensing / mobile-phone data -> forward-looking flood probability scenarios via my spatiotemporal data fusion pipeline
Green-infrastructure placement as a facility-location optimization problem -> extends my efficiency-equity EV-charging framework to flood-risk reduction
Urbanization-precipitation causal chain -> physics-informed graph learning with hydrological conservation priors, trained on WRF ensemble outputs
